mission.title: Install and Configure Jaeger on Kubernetes
mission.description: Jaeger is a distributed tracing platform that helps developers monitor and troubleshoot complex microservices architectures. Installing Jaeger allows you to visualize and analyze the performance of your applications, making it easier to identify bottlenecks and optimize your services.

steps.title: Add the Jaeger Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Jaeger Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add jaegertracing https://jaegertracing.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Jaeger using Helm
steps.description:
Install Jaeger in a new namespace called 'jaeger' with a specific version.
```bash
helm install jaeger jaegertracing/jaeger --namespace jaeger --create-namespace --version 2.15.1
```

steps.title: Verify Jaeger installation
steps.description:
Check that the Jaeger pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace jaeger
```

steps.title: Port forward to access Jaeger UI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Jaeger UI from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/jaeger-query --namespace jaeger 16686:16686
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration for resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Jaeger deployment to set resource limits for better performance.
```bash
kubectl patch deployment jaeger-collector --namespace jaeger -p '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"jaeger-collector","resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"512Mi"}}}]}}}}' 
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Jaeger will show all pods running in the 'jaeger' namespace. You can access the Jaeger UI at http://localhost:16686 to start monitoring your applications.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add jaegertracing https://jaegertracing.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Jaeger release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall jaeger --namespace jaeger
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that no leftover resources remain in the cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd jaegers.jaegertracing.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace jaeger
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Jaeger namespace to clean up all remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace jaeger
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your Jaeger installation. You can export the current configuration and any important data.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace jaeger -o yaml > jaeger-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version of Jaeger. Replace '2.15.1' with the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade jaeger jaegertracing/jaeger --namespace jaeger --version 2.15.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Jaeger pods are running the new version and are healthy.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace jaeger
kubectl describe deployment jaeger-collector --namespace jaeger
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that the Jaeger pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that may indicate the cause. You can view the logs using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace jaeger
```

troubleshooting.title: Jaeger UI not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Jaeger UI, ensure that the port forwarding is correctly set up. Check if the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace jaeger
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If Jaeger is not performing well, it may be due to insufficient resources. Check the resource limits and requests set for the Jaeger deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment jaeger-collector --namespace jaeger
```

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to CRDs, ensure that they are properly installed. You can check the existing CRDs with:
```bash
kubectl get crd
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
